utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, data-driven diabetes risk reports that satisfy all six judge evaluation criteria. Both read the actual data files, computed numeric 10-year risk estimates, decomposed contributing factors with specific values, traced the glucose trend, linked behavioral patterns to biomarkers, and provided quantified action plans. The wi_skills agent explicitly invoked the /ai-analyzer skill and its output is slightly more concise and tightly structured. The wo_skills agent produced a longer report with an arguably stronger final section that explicitly quantifies post-intervention risk reduction (from ~45–60% down to ~20–30%), which is a notable additional analytic step. Overall, the two outputs are very similar in quality and depth; wo_skills is marginally more comprehensive in its risk-reduction projection, while wi_skills is more concise. Neither clearly dominates — both are high quality, with wi_skills having the slight edge in following the intended workflow (using the skill) and wo_skills having a slight edge in quantifying the impact of recommended interventions.

utility_details.judge_error: wi_skills run failed verifier check V5 (medical disclaimer) — however, the report actually contains an 'Important Note' section stating it is for informational use only and not a diagnosis, which effectively serves as a disclaimer. The verifier may have looked for a specific keyword like 'disclaimer' and missed the equivalent language. No major runtime errors detected in either run.
utility_details.utility_summary: Both wi_skills and wo_skills produced high-quality, comprehensive health reports that passed nearly all evaluation criteria. Both correctly computed PSQI-based sleep quality assessments, identified PHQ-9 trends with clinical classification, quantified RDA gaps for iron and B12, constructed cross-domain causal narratives, prioritized risks with explicit rationale, and updated the ai-history.json with structured session entries. The wo_skills report is slightly larger (12180 vs 10640 bytes), passed all 5 verifier checks (vs 4 for wi_skills), and included a more explicitly labeled 'Medical Disclaimer' section. The wi_skills report used the /ai-analyzer skill invocation and produced equally substantive content. Overall, the two runs are nearly equivalent in quality across all judge evaluation items, with wo_skills having a marginal edge in format compliance (medical disclaimer labeling) and report length. The skill (wi_skills) did not provide a clear additional discriminating advantage in this case, as the baseline agent (wo_skills) also read all data files directly and synthesized them with equal depth.
